stem: Using the definition \( f'(x)=\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h} \) for \( f(x)=[[p]]x^{2}+([[q]])x+([[r]]) \), find \( f'([[x0]]) \).

Using the definition \( f'(x)=\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h} \) for \( f(x)=[[p]]x^{3}+([[q]])x \), expand \( (x+h)^{3} \), cancel the terms without an \( h \), divide by \( h \), and let \( h\to 0 \) to find \( f'([[x0]]) \).
solution: Expanding \( (x+h)^3=x^3+3x^2h+3xh^2+h^3 \), the difference quotient reduces to \( 3x^2+3xh+h^2 \) plus the constant term's slope \( [[q]] \); letting \( h\to 0 \) gives \( f'(x)=3[[p]]x^2+([[q]]) \), so \( f'([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the linear term's contribution \( q \).
This evaluates \( f(x_0) \) itself rather than the limiting difference quotient.
This uses the wrong power on \( x_0 \), as if differentiating \( x^2 \) instead of \( x^3 \).
This drops the exponent from the power rule, using \( x_0^2 \) with no leading coefficient of 3.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Using the definition \( f'(x)=\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h} \) for \( f(x)=\dfrac{1}{x+[[c]]} \), combine the two fractions over a common denominator before taking the limit to find \( f'([[x0]]) \).
solution: Combining fractions, \( \frac{1}{x+h+[[c]]}-\frac{1}{x+[[c]]} = \frac{-h}{(x+h+[[c]])(x+[[c]])} \); dividing by \( h \) and letting \( h\to 0 \) gives \( f'(x)=-\frac{1}{(x+[[c]])^{2}} \), so \( f'([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that comes from the numerator of the combined fraction.
This forgets to square the denominator.
This uses \( x_0 \) in place of \( x_0+c \) in the denominator.
This uses \( x_0 \) instead of \( x_0+c \), keeping the correct sign but the wrong shift.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using the definition \( f'(x)=\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h} \) for \( f(x)=\sqrt{x+[[c]]} \), multiply the difference quotient by its conjugate before taking the limit to find \( f'([[x0]]) \).
solution: Rationalizing, \( \frac{\sqrt{x+h+[[c]]}-\sqrt{x+[[c]]}}{h} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{x+h+[[c]]}+\sqrt{x+[[c]]}} \); letting \( h\to 0 \) gives \( f'(x)=\frac{1}{2\sqrt{x+[[c]]}} \). Since \( [[x0]]+[[c]]=[[s]]^{2} \), this is \( f'([[x0]]) = \dfrac{1}{2\cdot[[s]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of 2 in the denominator from \( 2\sqrt{x+c} \).
This uses \( 4s \) instead of \( 2s \) in the denominator.
This reports \( \sqrt{x_0+c} \) itself instead of its reciprocal-times-a-half.
This doubles \( s \) instead of taking a reciprocal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
